channel.title: Harris FedBuzz Topic Scout
channel.description: Weekly short-form video topic shortlist for US federal employees. Retirement, financial planning, and FDR-tagged items (flagged for media segregation).

item.title: Is your federal job on the Schedule F list?
item.guid: more than 50 dem lawmakers demand more schedule f transparency
item.description:
Schedule F reclassification directly threatens job security for up to 8,000 career federal employees by stripping civil service protections…

Score: 8.4 (pass) · Pillar: retirement · Source type: news

Source headline: More than 50 Dem lawmakers demand more Schedule F transparency

Short-form evaluation: Schedule F reclassification directly threatens job security for up to 8,000 career federal employees by stripping civil service protections and converting them to at-will status — high personal stakes for any fed wondering if their position is on the list. Possibly good for walk-and-talk as a news reaction; consider whether the lack of public documentation (the core tension) can be conveyed without visuals or charts, since the 'we don't know who's affected' angle may need careful verbal framing.

Short-form fit: pass

Alternate angles:

  • Stakes / FOMO: 8,000 federal jobs just lost civil service protections — yours?
  • Deadline: Feds still don't know who Schedule F targets — that's the problem.
  • Myth-bust: Schedule F isn't just political — it strips career feds' due-process rights.

Question: Could my federal career job be reclassified as at-will under Schedule F?

Recommended frame: news_reaction

Framing: News frame if date checks out; otherwise explainer.

Caution: Administration has released minimal documentation; avoid stating specific agency lists as confirmed fact.

Efforts to understand the true scope of the recent executive order moving around 8,000 career federal jobs into Schedule Policy/Career, making their incumbents effectively at-will employees, have been stymied by the administration’s scant public documentation.

Read source article

item.title: Did a federal arbitrator just restore your telework rights?
item.guid: arbitrator restores telework and remote work at the forest service
item.description:
A federal arbitrator overturning a telework rollback at the Forest Service is a direct livelihood story — feds across agencies will want to…

Score: 8.4 (pass) · Pillar: career_security · Source type: news

Source headline: Arbitrator restores telework and remote work at the Forest Service

Short-form evaluation: A federal arbitrator overturning a telework rollback at the Forest Service is a direct livelihood story — feds across agencies will want to know if this sets a precedent for their own remote work arrangements. The story is self-contained enough to work as a walk-and-talk reaction without charts, though the host should be careful not to overstate how broadly the ruling applies beyond Forest Service employees.

Short-form fit: pass

Alternate angles:

  • Stakes / FOMO: Federal workers: an arbitrator just reversed a telework rollback.
  • Deadline: Forest Service feds — your remote work status may have just changed.
  • Myth-bust: Feds, management can't always just cancel telework unilaterally.

Question: What does the Forest Service arbitration ruling mean for federal telework rights?

Recommended frame: news_reaction

Framing: News frame if date checks out; otherwise explainer.

Caution: Ruling covers Forest Service specifically — avoid implying all federal agencies are equally bound.

Arbitrator restores telework and remote work at the Forest Service GovExec.com

Read source article

item.title: Are federal employees losing their 'for cause' job protections?
item.guid: the end of for cause trump removes eac commissioners days after supreme court ruling on presidential removal power
item.description:
The Supreme Court's rollback of 'for cause' removal protections is a direct livelihood threat for federal employees who assumed civil servi…

Score: 8.1 (pass) · Pillar: career_security · Source type: news

Source headline: The End of “For Cause?” Trump Removes EAC Commissioners Days After Supreme Court Ruling on Presidential Removal Power

Short-form evaluation: The Supreme Court's rollback of 'for cause' removal protections is a direct livelihood threat for federal employees who assumed civil service rules shielded their jobs — the EAC firings make it concrete and immediate. This is possibly good for a walk-and-talk news reaction: one ruling, one real-world consequence, a clear 'am I next?' hook. Consider whether the EAC-specific detail needs brief context so federal viewers outside that agency still feel the stakes.

Short-form fit: pass

Alternate angles:

  • Stakes / FOMO: Federal workers just lost a key job-security shield — here's what changed.
  • Deadline: SCOTUS ruling is already being used — federal employees, this is happening now.
  • Myth-bust: Feds, civil service rules do NOT protect you the way you think they do.

Question: Does the SCOTUS removal ruling put federal employees' jobs at risk?

Recommended frame: news_reaction

Framing: News frame if date checks out; otherwise explainer.

Caution: Do not overstate scope — ruling applies to certain appointees; career civil service protections differ; hedge carefully.

Days after the Supreme Court ended “for cause” protections, President Trump fired EAC commissioners. What does this mean for your job security?

Read source article

item.title: Are USPS postal workers about to lose their jobs over a cash crisis?
item.guid: postal service buys time before cash runs out seeks federal subsidy
item.description:
USPS is in a cash crunch and is now seeking a federal subsidy — that has direct job security stakes for postal workers covered under FERS, …

Score: 8 (pass) · Pillar: federal_workforce · Source type: news

Source headline: Postal Service buys time before cash runs out, seeks federal subsidy

Short-form evaluation: USPS is in a cash crunch and is now seeking a federal subsidy — that has direct job security stakes for postal workers covered under FERS, FEHB, and TSP. The financial cliff narrative is concrete enough for a short reaction without charts, though the subsidy request angle may need one sentence of context to land cleanly.

Short-form fit: pass

Alternate angles:

  • Stakes / FOMO: USPS is nearly out of cash — what does that mean for postal workers?
  • Deadline: USPS is buying time right now before the money runs out.
  • Myth-bust: Postal workers are federal employees — their agency going broke is not routine.

Question: Does the USPS cash crisis put postal workers' jobs and benefits at risk?

Recommended frame: news_reaction

Framing: News frame if date checks out; otherwise explainer.

Caution: Do not imply layoffs or benefit cuts are confirmed — subsidy request is still pending.

Postal Service buys time before cash runs out, seeks federal subsidy Yahoo Finance

Read source article

item.title: Are you a fed who lost your federal disability telework accommodation?
item.guid: judge tells doj to reinstate telework for two attorneys as their lawsuit proceeds
item.description:
Federal employees with disabilities who lost telework accommodations as part of the RTO push have direct livelihood stakes here — a court j…

Score: 7.9 (pass) · Pillar: fdr · Source type: news

Source headline: Judge tells DOJ to reinstate telework for two attorneys as their lawsuit proceeds

Short-form evaluation: Federal employees with disabilities who lost telework accommodations as part of the RTO push have direct livelihood stakes here — a court just forced DOJ to restore accommodations while a lawsuit proceeds, signaling that ADA obligations may limit agencies' RTO authority. Possibly good for a walk-and-talk news reaction; the core legal point (injunction, disability accommodation, RTO) can be stated in under 45 seconds without charts, though the host should avoid overstating how broadly this ruling applies.

Short-form fit: pass

Alternate angles:

  • Stakes / FOMO: Feds with disabilities may have legal ground to fight RTO orders.
  • Deadline: A court just acted — federal disability accommodations under review now.
  • Myth-bust: Federal RTO orders don't automatically override your disability accommodation rights.

Question: Can federal agencies revoke disability telework accommodations under RTO mandates?

Recommended frame: news_reaction

Framing: News frame if date checks out; otherwise explainer.

Caution: Ruling applies to two specific employees — avoid implying it sets binding precedent for all feds.

A federal judge granted a preliminary injunction for two federal employees with disabilities who are suing DOJ for rescinding their telework accommodations.

Read source article

item.title: Postal workers — USPS bought more time, but is your job safe?
item.guid: postal service says its cash crisis is delayed until at least 2031 but problems loom
item.description:
USPS's cash runway now extends to 2031, but the headline's 'problems loom' framing directly touches postal workers' job security, pension c…

Score: 7.8 (pass) · Pillar: federal_workforce · Source type: news

Source headline: Postal Service says its cash crisis is delayed until at least 2031, but problems loom

Short-form evaluation: USPS's cash runway now extends to 2031, but the headline's 'problems loom' framing directly touches postal workers' job security, pension continuity under FERS, and long-term FEHB access. Possibly good for a walk-and-talk news reaction — the tension between 'crisis delayed' and 'not solved' is a natural 60-second beat, though the presenter must resist overreaching on specifics the snippet doesn't confirm.

Short-form fit: pass

Alternate angles:

  • Stakes / FOMO: USPS isn't broke yet — but postal workers should pay attention.
  • Deadline: 2031 sounds far off — but USPS says problems are already forming.
  • Myth-bust: The USPS cash crisis isn't over — the deadline just moved.

Question: What does USPS's delayed cash crisis mean for postal workers' job security?

Recommended frame: news_reaction

Framing: News frame if date checks out; otherwise explainer.

Caution: Do not imply layoffs or benefit cuts are imminent — the source only confirms delayed insolvency.

Postal Service says its cash crisis is delayed until at least 2031, but problems loom NPR

Read source article

item.title: Are you a fed waiting on a reasonable accommodation telework request?
item.guid: nteu sues treasury hhs over backlog of reasonable accommodation telework requests
item.description:
Federal employees with disabilities at Treasury and HHS face a concrete, personal stakes issue: their reasonable accommodation telework req…

Score: 7.6 (pass) · Pillar: fdr · Source type: news

Source headline: NTEU Sues Treasury, HHS over Backlog of ‘Reasonable Accommodation’ Telework Requests

Short-form evaluation: Federal employees with disabilities at Treasury and HHS face a concrete, personal stakes issue: their reasonable accommodation telework requests are sitting in a backlog, potentially forcing them back to an office they legally may not have to report to. NTEU filing suit signals this is escalating, not resolving quietly. Possibly good for walk-and-talk as a news reaction — consider whether the ADA/accommodation angle can land clearly in 30-45 seconds without requiring legal explanation.

Short-form fit: pass

Alternate angles:

  • Stakes / FOMO: Feds with disabilities may be forced back to office illegally.
  • Deadline: Treasury and HHS feds: your accommodation request may be stalled in court.
  • Myth-bust: Federal agencies don't get to ignore your reasonable accommodation request forever.

Question: What does the NTEU lawsuit mean for federal employees waiting on accommodation telework?

Recommended frame: news_reaction

Framing: News frame if date checks out; otherwise explainer.

Caution: Avoid implying all feds are covered — this targets Treasury and HHS employees specifically.

NTEU Sues Treasury, HHS over Backlog of ‘Reasonable Accommodation’ Telework Requests FEDweek

Read source article

item.title: Are you a federal Forest Service fed forced back to the office?
item.guid: arbitrator orders forest service to roll back return to office requirements
item.description:
Forest Service employees under return-to-office mandates have a concrete win here — an arbitrator ruling that RTO requirements must be roll…

Score: 7.5 (pass) · Pillar: career_security · Source type: news

Source headline: Arbitrator orders Forest Service to roll back return-to-office requirements

Short-form evaluation: Forest Service employees under return-to-office mandates have a concrete win here — an arbitrator ruling that RTO requirements must be rolled back is a direct livelihood stake with 'am I affected?' energy. Possibly good for a walk-and-talk news reaction, though the host should stay tight on what the ruling means for feds currently commuting or relocating, without drifting into labor law procedure.

Short-form fit: pass

Alternate angles:

  • Stakes / FOMO: Forest Service feds just won a fight against RTO mandates.
  • Deadline: Forest Service RTO rollback ordered — what happens to feds now?
  • Myth-bust: Federal agencies can't just ignore arbitration rulings on RTO.

Question: What does the arbitrator's RTO rollback ruling mean for Forest Service feds?

Recommended frame: news_reaction

Framing: News frame if date checks out; otherwise explainer.

Caution: Do not overextend ruling to all federal agencies — scope is Forest Service only.

Arbitrator orders Forest Service to roll back return-to-office requirements Federal News Network

Read source article

item.title: Are you a USPTO fed who lost telework without any bargaining?
item.guid: arbitrator rules uspto violated federal labor law by eliminating non patents employee telework without bargaining
item.description:
USPTO non-patents employees lost telework without the agency bargaining first — an arbitrator just ruled that violated federal labor law, w…

Score: 7.5 (pass) · Pillar: career_security · Source type: news

Source headline: Arbitrator Rules USPTO Violated Federal Labor Law by Eliminating Non-Patents Employee Telework Without Bargaining

Short-form evaluation: USPTO non-patents employees lost telework without the agency bargaining first — an arbitrator just ruled that violated federal labor law, which directly affects working conditions and could force telework reinstatement. The ruling has clear livelihood stakes for federal employees at USPTO and sets a precedent others may cite; possibly good for a walk-and-talk reaction if the host keeps it to the core question of what the ruling means for affected feds without getting into arbitration procedure.

Short-form fit: pass

Alternate angles:

  • Stakes / FOMO: USPTO feds: an arbitrator just said your telework was taken illegally.
  • Deadline: USPTO ruling is fresh — what happens to federal telework now?
  • Myth-bust: Federal agencies can't just cancel telework — this ruling proves it.

Question: What does the USPTO arbitration ruling mean for federal employees' telework rights?

Recommended frame: news_reaction

Framing: News frame if date checks out; otherwise explainer.

Caution: Ruling covers non-patents employees only — avoid implying it applies to all USPTO or all federal workers.

Arbitrator Rules USPTO Violated Federal Labor Law by Eliminating Non-Patents Employee Telework Without Bargaining IPWatchdog.com

Read source article

item.title: Is the USPS ground-transit shift putting postal jobs at risk?
item.guid: audit finds savings but issues with usps emphasis on ground transit
item.description:
The audit touches USPS operations and could ripple into postal worker job security if ground-transit shifts affect routes, staffing, or fac…

Score: 8.6 (pass) · Pillar: federal_workforce · Source type: news

Source headline: Audit Finds Savings, but Issues, with USPS Emphasis on Ground Transit

Short-form evaluation: The audit touches USPS operations and could ripple into postal worker job security if ground-transit shifts affect routes, staffing, or facility roles — but the snippet is thin on worker-level stakes. Consider whether a 30-45 second walk-and-talk can carry this without needing the full audit findings in hand; the hook potential is there if the filing details connect to postal workforce changes.

Short-form fit: review

Alternate angles:

  • Stakes / FOMO: USPS saved money on ground transit — but postal workers may pay.
  • Deadline: Auditors flagged USPS cargo issues — what happens to postal jobs now?
  • Myth-bust: Cheaper isn't always safer — what the USPS ground-transit audit really found.

Question: Does the USPS ground-transit audit signal risk for postal workers?

Recommended frame: news_reaction

Framing: News frame if date checks out; otherwise explainer.

Caution: Snippet is partial — confirm audit scope touches workforce before filming job-security claims.

The Postal Service has achieved some savings from an emphasis on ground transportation over air transportation but an air cargo ...More The article Audit Finds Savings, but Issues, with USPS Emphasis on Ground Transit was first published on FEDweek .

Read source article

item.title: Did your federal agency just rack up an $11 billion bill?
item.guid: deferred resignation price tag tops 11 billion as opm defends federal workforce cuts
item.description:
The $11 billion deferred resignation figure carries real stakes for federal employees wondering whether the buyout program was fiscally def…

Score: 8.2 (pass) · Pillar: retirement · Source type: news

Source headline: Deferred Resignation Price Tag Tops $11 Billion as OPM Defends Federal Workforce Cuts

Short-form evaluation: The $11 billion deferred resignation figure carries real stakes for federal employees wondering whether the buyout program was fiscally defensible and what OPM's defense signals about future workforce cuts. The retirement pillar angle is thin here — this is more a job-security and workforce-reduction story than a FERS or annuity explainer. Possibly good for a walk-and-talk news reaction if the host can anchor the 'what this means for remaining feds' angle, but consider whether the $11B figure alone sustains 30-45 seconds without budget charts or policy context the format can't carry.

Short-form fit: review

Alternate angles:

  • Stakes / FOMO: OPM spent $11 billion cutting the federal workforce — now what?
  • Deadline: Federal deferred resignation costs are in — feds need to hear this.
  • Myth-bust: The federal buyout wasn't cheap — $11 billion says otherwise.

Question: What does the $11 billion deferred resignation cost mean for federal workers still on the job?

Recommended frame: news_reaction

Framing: News frame if date checks out; otherwise explainer.

Caution: Avoid implying FERS retirement benefits are directly reduced; this is a workforce cost story, not an annuity cut.

Deferred Resignation Price Tag Tops $11 Billion as OPM Defends Federal Workforce Cuts FEDmanager

Read source article

item.title: Feds, OPM just killed the survey that tracked your workplace.
item.guid: opm dismantles centralized federal employee survey abandoning workplace accountability and transparency
item.description:
OPM canceling the Federal Employee Viewpoint Survey removes a data layer feds and agency watchdogs used to flag toxic workplaces and staffi…

Score: 7.9 (pass) · Pillar: career_security · Source type: news

Source headline: OPM Dismantles Centralized Federal Employee Survey, Abandoning Workplace Accountability and Transparency

Short-form evaluation: OPM canceling the Federal Employee Viewpoint Survey removes a data layer feds and agency watchdogs used to flag toxic workplaces and staffing problems — real stakes for civil servants who relied on it as a formal grievance signal. The story is abstract enough that a walk-and-talk risks turning into a lecture; consider whether a reaction format with a concrete 'what does this mean for you' payoff can carry it without visuals or data tables.

Short-form fit: review

Alternate angles:

  • Stakes / FOMO: Federal workers just lost a key workplace accountability tool.
  • Deadline: OPM's survey is gone — federal employees get no warning.
  • Myth-bust: Federal employee surveys weren't optional — OPM just made them disappear.

Question: What does OPM ending the federal workplace survey mean for civil servants?

Recommended frame: news_reaction

Framing: News frame if date checks out; otherwise explainer.

Caution: Partnership for Public Service is an advocacy source; note framing and check OPM primary confirmation before filming.

OPM Dismantles Centralized Federal Employee Survey, Abandoning Workplace Accountability and Transparency Partnership for Public Service

Read source article

item.title: Could your federal job lose its civil service protections?
item.guid: democrats demand transparency from white house on schedule policy career
item.description:
Schedule Policy/Career directly threatens civil service protections for federal employees, making this a genuine livelihood story — reclass…

Score: 7.9 (pass) · Pillar: retirement · Source type: news

Source headline: Democrats demand transparency from White House on Schedule Policy/Career

Short-form evaluation: Schedule Policy/Career directly threatens civil service protections for federal employees, making this a genuine livelihood story — reclassification could strip career feds of appeal rights and job security. The topic has real stakes but hinges on a political letter, not a concrete policy change yet, so the 'am I affected?' payoff is uncertain. Consider whether the walk-and-talk can land a clear takeaway without sliding into procedural process explanation.

Short-form fit: review

Alternate angles:

  • Stakes / FOMO: Schedule Policy/Career could strip career feds of appeal rights.
  • Deadline: Democrats are demanding answers now — before the list expands.
  • Myth-bust: Schedule Policy/Career isn't just for political appointees — career feds qualify.

Question: Could Schedule Policy/Career expand and strip your federal civil service protections?

Recommended frame: news_reaction

Framing: News frame if date checks out; otherwise explainer.

Caution: No final policy announced yet — frame as developing; avoid stating reclassification is imminent.

A letter sent to the White House pushes for details on Schedule Policy/Career and if there are plans to expand the list of those stripped of their protections.

Read source article

item.title: Are DOJ federal employees watching another FOIA leader walk out?
item.guid: doj s top foia official is stepping down
item.description:
The OIP director departure follows a wave of DOJ terminations, raising real questions for federal employees about FOIA office stability and…

Score: 7.9 (pass) · Pillar: career_security · Source type: news

Source headline: DOJ’s top FOIA official is stepping down

Short-form evaluation: The OIP director departure follows a wave of DOJ terminations, raising real questions for federal employees about FOIA office stability and career security inside DOJ. The personal stakes exist but are concentrated in a narrow slice of the federal workforce — DOJ and agency FOIA staff — which limits broad audience pull. Consider whether the walk-and-talk can land clearly without explaining what OIP is, since that context eats into the 30-45 second window.

Short-form fit: review

Alternate angles:

  • Stakes / FOMO: DOJ's top FOIA office just lost its second director fast.
  • Deadline: Federal FOIA staff: who is leading your office right now?
  • Myth-bust: This DOJ exit is not just a title change for federal workers.

Question: What does the OIP director departure mean for DOJ federal employees?

Recommended frame: news_reaction

Framing: News frame if date checks out; otherwise explainer.

Caution: Avoid implying the departure was involuntary — source says stepping down, not fired.

Glendening took over the OIP role last year after longtime Director Bobak Talebian was fired as part of a wave of DOJ terminations.

Read source article

item.title: Feds fired by State — are they first in line to be rehired?
item.guid: state dept is looking to rebuild after major staffing cuts laid off diplomats say they re not welcome back
item.description:
Laid-off federal diplomats are watching the agency that cut them now post the same roles — raising real questions about reinstatement right…

Score: 7.9 (pass) · Pillar: retirement · Source type: news

Source headline: State Dept is looking to rebuild after major staffing cuts. Laid-off diplomats say they’re not welcome back

Short-form evaluation: Laid-off federal diplomats are watching the agency that cut them now post the same roles — raising real questions about reinstatement rights, rehire priority, and whether prior federal service counts toward FERS if they were separated. The retirement pillar angle is thin here unless the video focuses on how a break in federal service affects FERS creditable service or TSP vesting, which could feel stretched; consider whether the job-security and rehire-rights angle carries the video without forcing a retirement hook.

Short-form fit: review

Alternate angles:

  • Stakes / FOMO: State cut federal diplomats — now it wants them back, maybe.
  • Deadline: Laid-off feds: reinstatement rights don't last forever.
  • Myth-bust: Federal rehire priority doesn't mean you're actually first called.

Question: Do laid-off federal diplomats have priority rights for these new State Dept postings?

Recommended frame: news_reaction

Framing: News frame if date checks out; otherwise explainer.

Caution: Reinstatement eligibility rules vary by separation type — avoid stating specifics without sourcing OPM guidance.

One year after sweeping layoffs, the State Department is hiring again for many of the same roles, leaving former employees questioning the agency's strategy.

Read source article
